block.name: opiates
block.caption: Opiates
Occur naturally in opium derived from poppy plant
Papaver somniferum
Thebaine
Codeine
Morphine
Oxycodone
Heroin
Synthetic

screen.name: screenA1
screen.text:
Oxycodone is a powerful ANALGESIC (pain reliever) prescribed to treat moderate to severe pain.
It belongs to a group of substances called OPIATES.

screen.name: screenA2
screen.text: Opiates are chemicals that naturally occur in opium.

screen.name: screenA3
screen.text: There are three different opiates: thebaine, codeine, and morphine.

screen.name: screenA4
screen.text: Oxycodone is a synthetic drug that is made from thebaine. Another drug derived from opiates is heroin, which is made from morphine.

screen.name: screenA5
screen.text: When a person is in pain, the body also produces its own analgesics (painkillers).

screen.name: screenA6
screen.text: These body-produced painkillers are very similar to opiates and the drugs made from them, oxycodone and heroin.
screen.caption: Body-produced painkillers
Produced in the brain in reaction to pain

screen.name: screenA7
screen.text: Because of their similarity, they are grouped together under the name OPIOIDS.
screen.caption: Body-produced painkillers
Produced in the brain in reaction to pain
All of these are opioids

screen.name: screenA8
screen.text: In addition to help overcome pain, body-produced painkillers are also responsible for the pleasurable, relaxing feelings we experience when we laugh or exercise.
screen.caption: Body-produced painkillers
Produced in the brain in reaction to pain
Produced when laughing or exercising

screen.name: screenB1
screen.text: When a person is physically hurting, the brain generates its own painkillers to relieve the pain.

screen.name: screenB2
screen.text: The same body-produced painkillers are released when a person exercises or laughs. The chemicals then don't act as painkillers, but induce feelings of pleasure and happiness.

screen.name: screenB3
screen.text: Body-produced painkillers act on many different structures in the brain and spinal cord to fight pain. However, to produce feelings of pleasure, they act on the brain's

screen.name: screenB4
screen.text: The reward system is a collection of different brain structures that work closely together.
screen.caption: Reward system

screen.name: screenB5
screen.text: Body-produced painkillers stimulate the reward system, which then "rewards" the person by producing feelings of pleasure.
screen.caption: Reward system
Body-produced painkillers

screen.name: screenB6
screen.text: Synthetic painkillers such as oxycodone can also act on the reward system.
screen.text2: The brain cannot distinguish between the two.
screen.caption: Reward system
Body-produced painkillers
Oxycodone

screen.name: screenB7
screen.text: Thus, in addition to fighting pain, oxycodone also produces feelings of pleasure when acting on the reward system.
screen.caption: Reward system
Body-produced painkillers
Oxycodone

screen.name: screen1
screen.text: The body produces its own painkillers to overcome pain. There is always a certain normal level of these painkillers present in the brain.
screen.caption: Click button to see what happens if somebody is in pain

screen.name: screen2
screen.text: If a person is in pain, the brain produces more painkillers.

screen.name: screen3
screen.text: The effects of the painkillers in the brain help to lower the feeling of pain so it’s not so intense.

screen.name: screen4
screen.text: As the pain is reduced and more manageable, the body decreases its production of painkillers until a normal level is reached. This process is called

screen.name: screen5
screen.text: Once the body’s natural painkillers return to normal levels, the person is usually okay and can deal with small amounts of pain.

screen.name: screen6
screen.text: What if the pain is so strong that body-produced painkillers are not enough?
screen.caption: Click button to start pain

screen.name: screen7
screen.text: Again, the brain produces more painkillers to help the body reduce the pain. However, this time the pain is stronger than the body can handle. A doctor prescribes oxycodone.

screen.name: screen8
screen.text: When a person in pain takes oxycodone, it adds to the effect of the high level of painkillers already produced by the body because oxycodone is so similar to them. This helps the body to overcome pain more than with body-produced painkillers alone.

screen.name: screen9
screen.text: As the pain is reduced, the person can stop taking oxycodone, and the body decreases its production of painkillers again until a normal level is reached.

screen.name: screen10
screen.text: Now, the person is okay with a normal level of body-produced painkillers.

screen.name: screen11
screen.text: Some people take advantage of the relaxing feelings oxycodone produces and take the drug to get high, without being in pain.

screen.name: screen12
screen.text: In a healthy person, the level of body-produced painkillers is normal. Oxycodone is added on top of that. For a short time, the person feels high.

screen.name: screen13
screen.text: However, the brain adapts by decreasing the production of its own painkillers, because without pain, the brain doesn’t need more than the constant level of painkillers.

screen.name: screen14
screen.text: As long as the person takes oxycodone, the level of painkillers in the brain is normal. However, they would not feel a “high” anymore. But what if the oxycodone intake is stopped?

screen.name: screen15
screen.text: When the person stops taking oxycodone, the brain ends up with a very low level of body-produced painkillers.

screen.name: screen16
screen.text: If the level of the body’s own painkillers is low, oxycodone will not produce a “high” anymore. A person might feel the urge to take oxycodone to just feel normal again.

screen.name: screen17
screen.text: However, if a person continues to abuse oxycodone, the body gets used to it. The person needs to take larger amounts of oxycodone to get the same effect initially felt from a smaller dose. This is known as TOLERANCE.

screen.name: screen18
screen.text: If the person stops taking oxycodone, they can experience symptoms of

screen.name: screen19
screen.text: The person might CRAVE more oxycodone and abuse the drug compulsively, despite negative consequences. Withdrawal and craving are signs of

screen.name: screen20
screen.text: Many changes in the brain can occur as one becomes addicted to oxycodone, and that’s why addiction is called a disease of the brain.

screen.name: screen21
screen.text: Oxycodone is a nervous system depressant, meaning it slows down the nervous system. Since the brain controls basic functions such as heart rate and breathing, these functions are slowed too.

screen.name: screen22
screen.text: Overdosing oxycodone can slow breathing so much that it stops altogether, resulting in death.
